finding: Four near-identical typed-'yes' confirmation blocks (input + strip + != 'yes' -> Cancelled + EOFError/KeyboardInterrupt handling) are copy-pasted across destructive CLI ops.
proposed_action: Introduce ONE shared confirmation seam (e.g. confirm_destructive(...)) that all four call, replacing the duplicated input/parse/cancel logic. Preserve exact semantics (typed 'yes', Cancelled on mismatch/EOF/KeyboardInterrupt, dry_run/force/confirm gating).

finding: Confirmation styles diverge: destructive ops use 'Type yes'; move/metadata prompts use [Y/n]/[y/N]; --clear-history has NO confirmation at all; irreversibility warnings appear ad hoc (some print a red IRREVERSIBLE line, clean-backups does not).
proposed_action: The shared helper standardizes the destructive-confirmation UX (consistent 'Type yes', consistent irreversibility emphasis). Bring --clear-history under it (add a confirmation).

finding: Each destructive op hand-rolls its own 'what will be affected' preview (rows/files/backups) with no shared way to show the FULL outcome (keep vs remove) or a forceful total-loss warning. This is why the clean-backups request (KEEP/DELETE + all-deleted warning) has no home to plug into.
proposed_action: Design a small preview+confirm abstraction: a data model for 'items to remove / items to keep / totals / irreversible?' and a renderer (color-independent KEEP/DELETE, forceful all-affected warning) + the confirm seam (ARCH-1). Each op supplies its items; the helper renders + confirms uniformly.
disposition: propose-now (this is the abstraction the user asked to design)

finding: The TUI has its own separate typed-'yes' safety modals (DeletionSafetyModal, ProjectDeletionSafetyModal) unrelated to the CLI confirmation code; a CLI-only helper will not unify them, and forcing a shared abstraction across a synchronous CLI prompt and an async Textual modal risks over-abstraction.
proposed_action: Keep the shared helper CLI-first. Extract the PURE parts (the preview *data model* + the color-independent *renderer/summary string*) so BOTH the CLI prompt and the TUI modal can reuse the render/summary without sharing the (sync vs async) confirmation mechanism. Do NOT unify the confirm I/O across CLI and TUI.
disposition: propose-now (split pure render from I/O; defer I/O unification)

finding: Each op independently interprets dry_run/force/confirm; a shared helper should take these explicitly so the gating is consistent and testable, not re-derived each call.
proposed_action: The confirm seam takes explicit (dry_run, assume_yes/force, interactive) inputs and returns a decision; ops keep owning WHAT to do, the helper owns HOW to confirm.

finding: A destructive-confirmation abstraction could balloon (plugin renderers, styles, i18n) beyond need. ocman is a single-maintainer local tool.
proposed_action: Constrain scope: ONE module-level helper + one small dataclass for the preview, in ocman.py (no new package, no new dependency). No config-driven rendering. Adopt in clean-backups first; migrate other ops incrementally, not big-bang.

area: anti-regression: refactor must preserve exact confirm behavior
finding: Refactoring 4 live destructive-op confirmations risks subtly changing when an op proceeds vs cancels (a safety-critical invariant).
proposed_action: Before migrating each call site, add characterization tests pinning current behavior: typed 'yes' proceeds; any other input/EOF/KeyboardInterrupt cancels; dry_run never prompts nor deletes; force/confirm=False bypasses prompt. Green before and after each migration.

finding: The pending clean-backups IPD (2026-07-04) proposes a bespoke KEEP/DELETE renderer; if the shared abstraction lands first, that IPD should consume the helper rather than implement its own, to avoid two divergent renderers.
proposed_action: Sequence: land the abstraction (this IPD) with clean-backups as its FIRST adopter; update/supersede the clean-backups IPD to build on the helper. Cross-reference both plans.
